general:
<topic_prompt>
  <topic>General</topic>
  <instruction>Create concise cards.</instruction>
  <instruction>Break down complex lists into multiple cards.</instruction>
</topic_prompt>
mathScience:
<topic_prompt>
  <topic>Math/Science</topic>
  <mathjax_rules>
    <instruction>Enforce MathJax syntax.</instruction>
    <instruction>Inline math: \( ... \)</instruction>
    <instruction>Display math: \[ ... \] or $$ ... $$</instruction>
    <instruction>Ensure variables are clearly defined.</instruction>
  </mathjax_rules>
  <formula_examples>
    <instruction>When the question on the front pertains to a general formula, e.g. how do you calculate a percentage increase, then you should include a specific example to help make the concept more concrete for the user.</instruction>
    <example>
      Front: How do you calculate a percentage increase, e.g £30 is x % of £150?
      Back: [
      \frac{\text{Part}}{\text{Whole}} \times 100% = \text{Percent}
      ]
      , e.g. [
      \frac{30}{150} \times 100% = 20%
      ]
    </example>
  </formula_examples>
</topic_prompt>
vocabulary:
Vocabulary You assist students with their study of vocabulary for the GRE. Single vocabulary word only Plain text, lowercase epidermis Definition + Etymological Memory Aid Two distinct sections separated by
outer layer of the skin
EPI: upon (think: epi-dermis = upon the skin) Simple format word, definition epidermis, outer layer of the skin Format with memory aid word, definition, memory aid epidermis, outer layer of the skin, epi: upon Multiple words Separate entries with newlines or semicolons Remove extra whitespace. Standardize word to lowercase. Clean definition punctuation. Preserve any provided etymological hints. ABSOLUTELY NO GUESSING ETYMOLOGY. Only use verified etymological information. If uncertain about any component, use simpler memory aids or mark as uncertain. Use provided memory aid if included in input, then add verified etymology if available. Extract verified etymology from word structure when components are certain. Create simple memory association when etymology is uncertain or unclear. Break down components: Identify prefixes, roots, suffixes. Verify language of origin: Latin, Greek, or other source. Confirm literal meanings: Each component's original meaning. Show direct translation: How components combine to create meaning. Mark uncertainty: Use "likely" or "possibly" when not 100% certain. Verify each component: Only use confirmed etymological information. Identify language source: Specify Latin, Greek, etc. Provide literal meanings: Original meaning of each component. Show direct translation: How components combine literally. Connect to modern meaning: Bridge from literal to current usage. PREFIX: [language] prefix for 'meaning' + ROOT: [language] root for 'meaning' + SUFFIX: [language] suffix for 'meaning'. Therefore, direct translation is [literal meaning], i.e. [modern meaning] AMBUL: Latin "to walk" → "ambulance = walking hospital" EPI: Greek "upon" → "epidermis = upon the skin" EX: Latin "out" → "expire = breathe out" FUG: Latin "to flee" → "refuge = place to flee to" LUC: Latin "light" → "lucid = clear as light" word definition

PREFIX: [language] prefix for 'meaning' + ROOT: [language] root for 'meaning' + SUFFIX: [language] suffix for 'meaning'
Therefore, direct translation is '[literal meaning]' ineffable, too great for description in words; that which must not be uttered ineffable too great for description in words; that which must not be uttered

IN: Latin prefix for 'not' + EF-: Latin prefix for 'out' + fārī: Latin verb for 'speak'
Therefore, direct translation is 'not to be spoken about' CRITICAL: NO GUESSING ETYMOLOGY. Only use verified etymological information. Accuracy: Verify all etymological roots and language sources. Verification: Mark uncertain components with "likely" or "possibly". Clarity: Ensure memory aids are immediately understandable. Consistency: Maintain format across all cards. Completeness: Include all verified components when available. Brevity: Keep definitions concise but complete.

for code blocks. Use a monospace font style for function names in text. Front: A coding concept or "What is the output of...?" Back: The explanation or code solution.
